channel_title: AI News & Strategy Daily | Nate B Jones
title: 12 hacks for AI in Tech Job Searches: 5 things to avoid and 7 tips that make LLM's work for you
executive_summary: Nate B Jones emphasizes the critical dos and don'ts of using large language models (LLMs) in job applications. He warns against using LLMs to write resumes or answer interview questions, while highlighting their usefulness in role fit analysis and identifying missing keywords. This guidance is essential for beginners aiming to stand out in a competitive job market.
bullets: Do not use large language models to write your entire resume. This is crucial because a resume needs your personal touch and specific experiences that LLMs cannot replicate. It's like trying to bake a cake using only a store-bought mix — it may look good, but it lacks the unique flavor that comes from your own ingredients.

Avoid using LLMs to answer company questions during the application process. If you rely on LLMs, you risk sounding generic and disconnected from the company's culture. Think of it like using a pre-made speech at a wedding; it won't resonate as much as your heartfelt words.

LLMs can be helpful for role fit analysis and understanding distinct job descriptions. This means you can use them to dissect job postings and see how your skills align with what employers are looking for. It's like using a map to navigate a city; it helps you find the best route to your destination.

Use LLMs to identify missing keywords in your resume. This is important because many companies use automated systems to screen resumes, and missing keywords can lead to your application being overlooked. Imagine trying to enter a club without the right password; you won't get in without the correct terms.

LLMs can assist in overcoming writer's block when crafting your resume or preparing for interviews. They can provide prompts or ideas that help you articulate your thoughts more clearly. It's like having a brainstorming buddy who nudges you when you're stuck on a project.

LLMs can help you gain a high-level view of the industry and competitors. This knowledge is vital for tailoring your application and interview responses to show you understand the market. Think of it as studying the playbook before a big game; knowing your opponents can give you a strategic advantage.

Do not use LLMs to tune your resumes. Fine-tuning requires a personal touch that reflects your unique experiences and achievements. It's like trying to adjust a family recipe without knowing the original ingredients — you might end up with something unrecognizable.

Avoid using LLMs for interview preparation. Preparing for interviews requires understanding the nuances of the role and the company, which LLMs may not capture. It's like practicing for a dance performance without knowing the choreography; you won't perform well without the right moves.

LLMs can help you break through fluency blocks when discussing complex topics. They can provide clarity and structure to your thoughts, making it easier to communicate effectively. Think of it like using a guide to navigate a dense forest; it helps you find your way through the confusion.

Use LLMs to practice initial interview structures. This can help you become familiar with common interview questions and how to respond effectively. It's like rehearsing for a play; the more you practice, the more confident you become on stage.

Nate emphasizes that there are conflicting perspectives on using AI in job applications. This means you should critically evaluate advice and find what works best for your situation. It's like navigating a maze; not every path leads to the exit, so choose wisely.

Remember that LLMs are tools to assist you, not replace your efforts. Relying solely on them can lead to a lack of authenticity in your applications. Think of it like using a calculator for simple math; while it can help, you still need to understand the basics.
key_takeaways: DO avoid using LLMs to write your entire resume. This is essential because a resume needs to reflect your unique experiences and personality. Skipping this step could result in a generic resume that fails to impress employers.

DO use LLMs to identify missing keywords in your resume. This can significantly improve your chances of passing through automated screening systems. If you skip this, your application might be overlooked, even if you're qualified.

DO practice initial interview structures with LLMs. This will help you become more comfortable with common interview questions and improve your performance. If you don't practice, you may struggle to articulate your thoughts during the actual interview.

technical_terms.Large Language Models (LLMs): These are advanced AI systems designed to understand and generate human-like text. Think of it like a super-smart assistant that can help you write or brainstorm ideas.
technical_terms.Role Fit Analysis: This is the process of evaluating how well your skills and experiences match a specific job description. Like trying on clothes to see if they fit, this helps you determine if a job is right for you.
technical_terms.Keywords: These are specific terms or phrases that are important in job descriptions and resumes. Think of them like magic words that unlock doors in the hiring process.
technical_terms.Writer's Block: This is a condition where a writer struggles to produce new work or ideas. It's like being stuck in traffic; you know where you want to go, but something is blocking your path.
technical_terms.Fluency Blocks: These are obstacles that prevent you from expressing your thoughts clearly and confidently. Like a foggy day that obscures your vision, fluency blocks can make communication difficult.
